speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This first conversation with Eugene Settle was recorded at his home in Moscow on June 3, 1975.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I wanted to know some about your grandparents and where they came from. Well, of course my grandparents, I never did know my grandfather on my father's side. He passed away before I was born and I don't remember my grandmother on my father's side. She was alive when I was with her when I was born, but I don't remember she passed away. But my mother's father's parents, they lived in Mississippi, kind of impressive, right on that Tennessee border in Tennessee.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And my father's parents, they lived in North Carolina. That's where he was born, in North Carolina. And then they left North Carolina and came to Mississippi and that's where my mother and father met. When they were in school together, they went in school together, my father went to one district and my mother went to another. And my mother went to the third. Where my dad went to school, he had a little longer school there and when school was out in my mother's district, my mother was one of the oldest one of the children and her and the next district where my dad was going to school at and that's where my dad and mother went for a month or whatever length of time, just to get in more school. Of course, I guess in those days they never had school in three or four months out of the year because in the year of 90 it was something. When I lived on the farm, we'd come here about six and seven months is all we went to school to when I was a kid, year 90 in the country schools. Because we were seldom, well once in a while we'd have seven months but not very often for a long time. Of course, in the later years, we did in my early school years, I was in eighth grade I guess, or seventh, sixth, seventh grade. We never went from about five, six months a year out of all the schools we had in the country schools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your parents' parents doing in Mississippi to get by when they were there and met?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, of course, my parents when they got married, of course they had one of my sisters, they had one of my sisters born, I don't know, I don't know what one of my, I guess my dad, I guess they was farming there, I don't know, but of course, after my sister was born, I was right why, they went out to the Indian Territory. So they just said to my sister, two years older than I am, and she was born in Mississippi, and I was born in Mississippi, but I don't know what married life they spent in Mississippi, I really don't know just what my father, I know he had a team. He was a family, I suppose he was farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it your mother's mother or your father's mother who was Indian?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My father's mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that meant that she had, she got land in your home?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's right, yeah, that's, you see in that, after the Civil War, the government decided to move all the Indians off of the East Coast, and they set aside some of the acres of land in the Oklahoma Territory, and they was going to give them so much land there, I don't remember now whether it was 20 or 40 acres, I really don't know just what she was. And they started moving, started resettling them out there on 40 acres. Well, they got to put all of them out until they got out to Louisiana, and they, of course, there was a tribe in - Where there was the Seminoles. Seminoles, the Seminoles, they never did make no treaty with the Seminoles, they were not able to get them, and of course, and at that time, my grandmother, she was married to my father and they moved out to Mississippi when this came up, see, and so therefore, she didn't, after they even, they didn't interfere with her anyway, you see, and then after they lived in Mississippi, raised a family, and then she decided to put in for her claim out in the Indian Territory, and finally she got it, I think, you gotta say, I don't remember if it was 40 acres or 20, she was like 40 though, I won't say for sure on that, and then when she got this 40 acres, her and my grandfather went out there, built a little home along those 40 acres, and then he passed away shortly after that, about a year or two after they got there, and by that time, my father was married, and then he went out there with his mother on those 40 acres, and that's where I was born, and in short, after I was born, well then, it was mostly rock, this 40 or 20, whatever it was, it was mostly rock, steel rock, steel rock, my dad told him, whatever steel rock is, I don't know what it is, but I know he talk about making oil and gas out of steel rock or something, but anyway, there was a very little farm land on it, so he had a good team, and obviously, he took off for work, to find work to make a living, so somebody told him about Fort Smith, Arkansas, and so it's not too full of land, so he and another fellow, he got in the wagon and left her mother out there, and that's the kid with his mother, and he left Fort Smith, Arkansas, and he got a job there, and he worked for, that's where I worked most of the time, he was in Oklahoma, and of course, he left the family there in Oklahoma, and then that's where I heard about the great northwest, the fellow that owned us, that he worked for, he and his wife ran a big store there, and they had, the railroad was completed then to the coast, and he and his wife took a trip, worked for Arkansas to Seattle, and he was so amazed about the open spaces across the state of Washington there, the open land there, that was for homestead, and so he told my dad, being a young man like he was, and leaving family, he thought if he was back here younger here, that's where he was to go, and so my dad, he was quite a, he liked to hunt, and this fellow told him he'd see lots of wild game from a train, you know, he didn't say what it was, and my dad, he just really wanted to do that, that's the place for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He took your father aside to tell him all about the trip?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yes, yeah, my dad was haulin' freight for him, that's why he was using his team, haulin' freight from the depot up to this big store, and he came back there on a loadin' freight there in the back of the store, and he came back there, and while dad was workin' and stole it, and says, tell him when he gets through, he says, I wanna talk to you, so he stepped there, and wait till dad got through, and so he had to come, that's what he was tellin' about, he'd bought this trip that he'd just got through makin' from Fort Smith to Seattle, and what he saw on the trip, and what he thought of the country and all that, and what a great chance that he thought it was for a young man out here to make a living, it was so much land to be picked up for homesteading.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father decided pretty soon after to come?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he does, he made up his mind then as soon as he'd get enough money together, he was gonna come, he would come west, and then in the meantime, there was another family by the name of Hampton, I think it married some relation to this store man, and they decided to come too, they were young fellas, and this is when the store, he said he was gonna try to induce his son to come out there and buy some of it, pick up some of that land, and this other family that was kinda related to this old man by marriage, they had some boys and they got it clear from my dad, in fact my dad leased some land down on Arkansas River out just out of town down there, his pasture land, and he leased it while he was working with this old fella, and he had a team, and he can take them down there on weekends and turn them out, and the pasture, you know, and so this part here, they had quite a few stocks, and so they got, my dad got this land, they got him the pasture stock for them, and so that's just why he got acquainted with them, and the dad told what he was figuring on doing as soon as he's coming west, and so they got quite excited about it, so they went in and talked to this old man, he worked for them about it, and he told him just what they thought was my dad's, it was a great place for young men, and these young fellas, they weren't married, they were just young fellas that weren't married at that time, three young fellas, and so they went back and their folks had quite a holding, they were farmers, and they talked their folks into letting them come out west, and they kept some of this group of fleece land, and so of course they had lots of stocks, horses, mules, and such as that, and so their old dad told them, well that's the way, you just, he gave them some horses, so I believe it was four head of horses, and I believe it was a cow,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and so they decided to ship the ship out here, and they got the cattle, and of course my dad, he just had the one team, wagon, so he kept his team, he kept his horse, he kept his, well he was working them anyway, but then when they decided to ship the cattle, they wanted to know through my dad if he wanted to, why didn't you go in and ship the ship together, and so that he would, he had to sell his team, he wanted to sell it, he sold it anyway so he'd have more money to get out here, and he sold his house, but he had, but he did go up into, back up to Indian Territory and loaded up, loaded up the whole family, and furniture and everything we had, and he shipped that out here, shipped, put it in the car, was there, shipped that out here, up to Bluestem Washington, and then I guess it was nearly a year later, why, that wasn't nearly, I don't know, I really don't know just how long the time it was, but anyway, it was quite a little while after that because we went from there on up to Mississippi, wait for my younger brother to be born, my mother was family, so she could take the trip, so we went from the high coast up to Mississippi there, I don't know how long we were there, but it was in there quite a little while, several months anyway, before we got started out here, before we got to go on the rest of our way, and then we came up here, and as I said before, we got shipped out to Moscow, Washington, and we got off the train, we was in Moscow, Idaho, and we stayed here three or four days, and some people tried to get my dad to stay here, they told him that he couldn't make it up, it would be awful hard for a man that's from the south that's been used to farming the way they do down here, and to come up here, and try to make it up there, and he said there was nothing up there but wild horses and jackrabbits and sagebrush,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and so there's a fellow that ran a restaurant down here, I believe it was just about by the Chinese restaurant down there on Main Street down there, right in there someplace, I believe it's the same place, I won't say for sure, but right in there in one of those places, and he told father that he had a couple of nephews that had a place rented out from town here, he'd brought them down, he rented the place for them, and he lived in Spokane, and he had his nephews up there, and because the kids, fellas, and so to get them out of town, he rented this place down there and rented it for them to farm for, and he said about another year, they had a year of age, and said they'd probably give up the place, they didn't like it anyway, and maybe he could get it, maybe my dad could rent it, if they gave it up, well my dad, he wasn't much interested in that, he wanted to go back home and get a home, get his own place, but anyway, after we decided to go back up to Moscow, he got this fellas address, so he could write to them if he did decide to give the place up, could get it dirty, anyway, and then of course this old fellow, he wanted to know, he actually wants to know how my dad's getting along, how he made it up here anyway, you know, and so we went up there, and he, father found us a homestead cabin out on Crab Creek, I don't know how many miles it was from Bluestem or Moscow at that time, but our stuff that we shipped out was our furniture, our bedding and everything was there, we'd been there for a long time, but it was there, so we got this cabin, our little house out on Crab Creek, and moved us out there and got all our stuff out and packed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a homestead? Huh? Was it a homestead?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well it had been, I think somebody had homesteaded and then abandoned that city, it was a nice little house on it, wasn't very big, but we laid out at it all right, and had the water running right down from the side, the yellow running water went right through the house, you know, from the spring, and then just about time we got settled, and my dad did a little getting around as much as he could, he didn't have nothing to ride, he had no way of traveling, but he inquired around, he got out, started looking around to find out if it wasn't finally, and his cattle, he ran onto a cattleman by the name of Warwick, and he gave him a job, in fact Warwick only had, he picked up the option on this place where we lived in, just in here, this homestead, and he brought a bunch of cattle through there, a lot of land, some of it was his, and a lot of it was just open country, and so dad went to work with him and got, he got a horse, and looked around, and he found a homestead, at that time, somebody else had got was four out, and there was very few families that had it, picked up homes, and no school, my sister was getting about ready for school, and I was, not too far off, and yes my sister was ready, and so he took that all into consideration, and then finally this fellow wrote to him, told him about this, we got upset, it was a small fish, and we got over that, and of course my dad, he was a little bit discouraged over that, but still we didn't, we all came through it all again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember having a smallpox?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I remember, I don't remember having something, I remember my little brother, that we waited to be born and come out here, I remember his face off, just like I remember seeing him, I don't remember how I looked, I felt easy, but I remember my little brother more than anybody, because he was just a little, how can I guess, six months old, little nursing baby, and he'd have blisters on his face, I remember that. And anyway, he got to look around for him, and all the way, there was no school, and then he got a letter from this fellow down here, this fellow wrote to him and told him that the boy is just going to give up, he is going to give up the place, and if he thought he was interested, well he would talk to the fellow along with it, and maybe put in a big, maybe he could get it. So he did that, so Dad wrote to him, wrote back to him and told him, well if he could lease the place, he thought he would come back to Moscow, so finally he got rid of this man, and told him that he talked to the man all the time, he gave Granddad a chance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know why your father decided not to stay here in the first place, why he wanted to push on?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he just thought it was better, he went to the home state, he wanted to get a place of his own, and of course he didn't have nothing, no more up there than he did here, but he was asking for it, but he just thought that he had heard so much about this country up there, and he wanted to go back up there and get himself a home state, and he figured that there would be some way of making it. But it turned out to be harder than he did in the first place. Yes, after he got up there, well then he got a job, went to work with this fellow, he was good to see the country, and looked to see what it was, and the exact fact that they had horses and stuff like that there, and it was a rather dry country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how many neighbors you had up there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I tell you, the more we were at it, we didn't have any neighbors. Not until we got to Moscow, the little village, we wasn't too many miles off where we were in, it wasn't too many miles off from this little town, Bluestem, Moscow then, but up and down the creek where we lived there, I don't know, there wasn't any neighbors around there, and I know of them. Of course there might have been some around I didn't see, but you know how it was inside of where we lived, I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, this was in 1898, is that right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It must have been about 1898 or 1999, I won't say the exact, well, I'll see, it must have been about 1998, because we got here, we came down here in 1999, I think we came down here, 1900, I don't know that time, I don't know, I want to assume at least, but right in there somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you, can you remember yourself, anything about the living there, when you were just, you must have been about five, in Washington?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I remember, I say I remember, my sister and I, we used to get sticks and make, take fish hooks out of pens and go down on the crab creek and try to fish, and I remember I caught a clawfish and I got him a pile of water and he fell off my pen and I sure was glad to see him go, I found all those big claws, and then we had out there in the flat along the creek there, we had a bird nest and we'd watch them, watch those bird nests and watch the little ones go, and one thing, I remember that, well, my sister and I, we just had a ball while we was there, running around, and of course mother watched us on, so many cattle, wild, some wild cattle, and de -cactus, and all that, where it hurt her to death, and we had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about getting by, and you know, talk about how hard it is for the people pioneering, was it really hard for your father to keep meat on the table, or did he even have meat to put on the table at all?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, well, I tell you, it seemed like that, I don't think we got hungry or not, but it seemed like we, we lived on codfish, you could get it in these blocks, these clothes, you know, and well, it's cooking, and I know we ate lots, I know we ate lots, and that must have been the cheapest thing to buy, I suppose, I know we ate lots of codfish, and I tell you, that spring, on the hill, the water ran right through the house, by running through the house, they had a kind of a, it wasn't a tub, but it was just kind of a big pan in there, you run into that, and then you run on out onto the house, and on down to the creek, you see, and you put a stopper in this pan there, let it get full of water, and that's, I don't remember, that's why we, the codfish, we put it in this pan here, let the water run down on it, and keep it cool, to save it, I don't know about that, that was our refrigerator. At that time. And what I don't remember, I know we had pretty, but I think, I think they'll, I do remember though, after we came to Idaho here, I know there was times that there wasn't very much on the table.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It was just about as bad as homes did, I imagine, it was, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any water problem, did that spring provide you with all the water?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, it ran all the time we was there. I think one of the, one of the fellows, the boy, he was a friend my dad worked for down there, he came up there and he did some work on it one time, but I don't know what he did, but I don't remember never stopping or something like that. I remember we was up to the spring, lots of time, my sister and I, they just, we grew up, I don't know how far it was, up on the hills from the house there and some willows or things up there, and he just, he dug out, kind of rocked up, it wasn't a cement, just rocked up.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: If I remember right, there wasn't even any, wasn't even any fence around her. I guess the cattle could go and drink out if they wanted to, if I remember right, but it seemed like it was a kind of a basin that all rocked up and pipe, and water, pipe out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, you said too that you remembered being at Pendleton on your way to the town?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's what I remember, I remember that better than anything else, it was kind of the vision for it at Pendleton. We came to Pendleton, Oregon, we was over there quite a little while, and around the station there, there was lots of Indians, and they was there, Indian regives, you know, feathers and all that, and I remember, I don't know if you remember, you see carts, wagons they have on the platform of the station where they push the luggage around with, I remember my dad was fiddlin' up again and sittin' on there and talkin', and we was there while we were talkin' to those Indian boys, fellas, and that there. I wonder what your dad was talkin' about. I just wonder, I don't know. Maybe about what the country was like. I imagine, I imagine. Well, you said the Indians in Oklahoma didn't have any regalia like - I never did see any regalia, of course, I wasn't, that might have seemed, I don't remember, but I don't, I don't, I don't remember. But that's, seems like that's the first thing I can remember, that it had all the regalia on, the feathers and all that. But I don't remember seeing any in Oklahoma, but I know as long as they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they seem like a real different, different kind of people than the people in Oklahoma?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I tell you, I don't know nothin' about the people in Oklahoma, I don't remember. I don't, I don't remember a thing in Oklahoma, I just, I was just too, too young when I left there. Oh, you've got - Hello. Oh, yeah, this is - Hi.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: How do you do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'm Sam Schrader. Just a - He had a little gray horse that he rode when he, the town, right. My sister said, oh no, that wasn't in Oklahoma, she said that wasn't in Arkansas. So that let me out, I don't remember, I don't remember nothin' about Oklahoma. No, that's true. And probably not of Mississippi either then, because that was a short time. No, that's a short time. Of course, I remember a little more about Mississippi while we was there, between my mother and my mother was the one I remember.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He lived in a log house, and he did most of the cooking on the fireplace.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And my grandfather had a banjo hanging up on the wall there, and I used to beg him to play it because he never would. And after I got the inside, my mother told me why he didn't play it, he said before.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: When he was a young man, he used to play the banjo and go to dances and he'd sing. Then he got married, except for Christ, and hung the banjo up, and that was actually on. Never touched it again, and he didn't. And as long as you remember, he never did touch it again. He hung it up there on the - never touched it again. But before that time when he was young, he'd go to - he was a musician around there, he'd go to a place and have dances, and he'd sing an old night at home. He got married, and except for Christ, he hung the banjo up, and that was it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And my dad went down - went back down there in 19 - I got out of service in 1919. We were winter of 19 - just the winter of 1919 I guess it was, when I got out of service in 1919, I think he and my mother went down there. And he went down there, so he spent the winter down there, and he - my mother was still living in the old house, and he - Well, when he tore it down anyway, he built her another little house, a house down there down there, I suppose. I don't remember, never thought to ask him about it, I never thought to ask him about the banjo, I suppose it was still hanging there. But I know my sister and I, we used to see it up there, I had a little bit of a fear of it. He kept it as a token of his - I guess so, I guess. Of his early days. Yeah, I guess so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whereabouts in Mississippi?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Corinth. It's on the - pretty much on the Tennessee border. Now, Corinth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, then when your father decided to come back to Moscow, Idaho here, one thing about that I was wondering was, did you ever know why this man was so good to your father, who he didn't really know?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he was a Negro man, he ran a restaurant down there, he was the only black business man in town, and some Chinese, and there was quite a few Chinese in Moscow at that time, but that's why he ran a little restaurant down there, that's why I said it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's why he took so much risk from his dad, and his dad was a young man, and he had a few or four little kids, and - What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Chrisman. Chrisman. His daughter was the first black student to ever graduate from the University of Idaho, and I think she wasn't in the first class, but she must have been in the second class, and never graduated up here on the University of Idaho.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: His daughter did, then running out here, and he - while he was running from - I think he was running from Chicago to Spokane. I don't know whether he was sleeping carpool or what, but anyway, he was a little old man. And while he was in Spokane, he picked up some mining stock up here, Coeur d 'Alene mines, some of the mining got, and eventually got to be pretty wealthy. Of course, they did now, and she - her son, in 1919, when I came back from the Army, she had a son that finished high school, and she sent him down here to go to the University of Idaho where she went. He come down here and went one first semester, and then the second semester he come to Oregon and got sick and died. That's 1919, so he kind of lost track of him after that, I don't know, whatever it came from. I don't know if she's a year old yet now, I don't know whether she had any more children or not, but I don't know.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This son, of course we met her, she came back here, well that was the one of my parents, was down south that way, so we met her when she come down here after his body. What kind of a store did Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Chrisman have?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Restaurants. You know, he ran a restaurant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know when he came?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't know, he was here when he came, I don't know how long he had been here before we got here. But he was here for quite a while afterwards. Yeah, well I don't remember that, because I was just a little kid, I just, I know he was here. I know he was here when we got here, and I remember the boys that farmed the place there, too, because of that, but they left. I don't know how long he left, when he just left that next year or not, I don't remember how long it was. But I know I heard fellas say for years after going at the old icebox, he had a kind of, his homemade one in the back of that building, and I know it was in that building for years after he left here, but I heard fellas say that the old icebox was still in there, and I guess it's worn out now, they talk about that. But whether he left right after we got here or not, I think though that he was around here a year or two after we got here. But I never...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his daughter much older than you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know, I never did see his daughter, I never did see them, I never did see any of them, but I see I saw him, I know he drove a nice horse to a single buggy, I see him and then his nephews, two big custody lads out on the place, they were out there for me when we got there, and after we got there they left, and I never did see them anymore, and I don't remember, I might have seen fish by the time they left, but I was little, I don't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now where was that place, the place where you lived and rented?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It was over here on the little potlatch, it's on the, it belonged to, it belonged to Postmaster's grandfather, Bill McKenna.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's the first place we rented, he farmed that for two or three years, and then he got a chance to buy this young man that's renting quite a bit of land, two different places, and he had about a hundred and some acres of fall wheat sold, he was a bastard, and he had a housekeeper, had some woman still keep a house for him, and he got hurt in trouble, and so they kept making hot for him, and he wanted to get out of the country, and so he finally sold his lease to my dad, my dad had just run all over the country, he was a stranger here, he wasn't here very long, and he raised money to buy this fella out, you know, he thought it was a good deal, and finally some guy out with, came to a rescue, let him have the money, to buy this fella out, and so he had a good crop the next year, and, Where was this place?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This and that over there, we're in the same country over there, south of, south of Joel,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and he did raise money, paid all of his debts, and bought 80 acres of timber, it was about 15 acres of farmland, no buildings on it though, and in that winter, he came down with a little bit of rheumatism, and for two months after that he was in bed all with rheumatism, so, set him back, but he kept on going,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and running around, and Do you remember how much, about how much that place, or did you know about how much it would cost in those days, the 80 acres?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't, I know it wasn't really much, I don't imagine it was over, because it was, it was what they called part of a school section land, I don't know, I don't imagine it cost to go about it, I doubt if it cost $20 an acre, maybe, I don't know, maybe, maybe $20, $25 an acre probably, but I don't know, I know it wasn't, wasn't, not compared to what it is now, of course in those days $20 was money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, your father had to make the, and he had to make the money by, to pay the place off, by the money that he made by renting, renting land, other land, and where did you live then, while you, while he was, there wasn't any place on the 80, right?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, no, no, no, we still, we still lived on the Buchanan place, we're still, we're still farming the Buchanan place when he bought this 80, and then after he bought this 80, though, then he rented another place that joined this 80, see, and then we moved on that, that's, that is over in that same country, but it was about four miles from the Buchanan place, and then that's why we, that's why we were there for a long time, that's why we were there for a long time, on that, on that place there for quite a long time. What kind of an outfit did he have as far as it, he didn't have, he didn't have, he didn't have nothing when we came here, we never had enough, no man of livestock at all, but he just, you finally, you know, there's a big fella over in the, you know where the main country is, of course, the one over there, a man by the name of Red, he was quite a, he had a lot, quite a bit of land over there, and he had a lot of cattle, and they were on that whole country where we were, that part of the fence, and a lot of, he hadn't more stock than anybody in the country, and they were on the wall over there where we were.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Would you folks like popcorn?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Mm -hmm. Oh sure. Oh, where we lived, and he had a lot of horses, Kai used to run in those, and so, and he had a son about the age of my dad, he got to be a real engineer.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: One of the real boys was a railroad contractor building railroads, and they got to, they used to pretty well run up to the street. And he had about eight wild horses, and unrope horses, and so, we just thought it was about the same age as my dad, he owned four of them, and he's kind of like my dad, they looked like his partner. And so he got two, I think dad, he traded them on a five -headed, those Kai uses wild horses, and the other one broke his range horses, with two of his. And that's why he got his starting line, he broke both horses, and that's why he got his starting line, and the horse made it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He traded your dad?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He traded with your dad?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, my dad traded with my dad, I don't care about his trade, he traded around,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: and he had to break them. Oh yes, he had to break them.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Of course, before he got these Mustangs, though, he had got a team from this, I guess these Christmas had them, they had, they owned a team, the boys had a team, I think he both got their team, I'm sure he did. Yeah, he got their team, so he had one team.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then he got these five Mustangs, and he got them broke.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And my dad, he was, he's quite a trader, he liked his horses and traded around, for what month, before he had any horses, and I don't remember when, I already got a hold of his first cattle, I don't remember when I already got a hold of him. I know that he and I, one Sunday, got on a horse, wanted to go over across the hill over here, because wherever Daniel Gammell had a place over there on this side of the lane, he over there, and we went over to his place looking at cattle, but we never got any from him, and I don't remember when he got his first cattle.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I don't know why we left every cattle place over on that, it was a crazy cow. It wasn't long before we had, because he had quite a bunch of cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have farming equipment too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yes, he bought this other place over there, he sold this, he bought this crop over there, and then he had to buy machinery, he bought a binder, that is to cut the grain, and then after that fall, I say he paid up, and then he rented this other place, I think, let's see, what was it, oh I'll tell you, Buchanan was coming back on the place himself, you know, he was a deputy marshal at that time, and he got out of the job, and he was coming back, moving back on his own farm, and that's, and then he rented this other place over there, but he paid pretty good that fall, and paid up, and then he bought his binder, and he bought him a plow, and other farming equipment, so he was in pretty good shape then.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: This was farming equipment going in horses, and I say it wasn't long before he accumulated quite a herd of cattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By how long do you think it took before he had the place paid off?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, he never did pay the place off. You see, some of the farmland was on this timber, was on this place that we moved on to. And of course he had that to farm, he had that to farm, rented it, and that was going to this, was on this thing, was in there, so when he got down from here, he had to let the place go back, see. You know, I was about seven years old, at one minute I didn't go to school all winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why's that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I stayed home and took care of this stock, and...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Help, mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that hard for you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, it was hard for me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You had a man's responsibilities. Yeah, I did the, I did the full work, I did the pawing, and I started, I started to feel pretty young.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then the next year, he rented a hundred and sixty, because it was in there south of, no, east of Blaine. Excuse me. That's not right, I didn't do it. East of Blaine, and then, then he, then the next one, he got the rheumatism again, and that spring come, he wasn't able to do no work, and, and he had a lot of that place to put in, spring crop, you know, it wasn't even thought. And then the neighbors all over the country came in, with their teams and claws, and, and gave him a day's work there, and they put him there to put that place in, in that one, one day. So many fellows come over to help him. But after they left, anyway, he got a fellow to drive his team when they finished it, but he didn't, his neighbors all over the neighborhood.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He had an air balloon, air balloon. He went around to all the neighbors all around the country, and got them to come over and give him, give my dad a day's work on that place over there, to get his chalk there, so they did. They came over, they just cleaned some water, and got a nice little laugh, like, there wasn't much left to do anyway, but, with his team, he got somebody to drive his team.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He got a copy, yeah, did pretty well. And he farmed over there for years, farmed over on that side of the mountain until about 1911.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he leased the place over here south of the cemetery, when a fellow by the name of Bessie owned it out there. And he farmed that two years, and he bought 20 acres out here northeast of Moscow. And he owned that until, I guess about three years ago, I sold it. That's where those kids grew up and went to school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Northeast of town. Yeah, northeast of town. If you've been out northeast, if you know where Bendel's Hatchery is out there, well, it's just north of that. If only you'd seen a house up there, and trees up there, I guess that place up there. I'll go home for you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, when he had rheumatism, he was just in the winter, and spraying the cot. It seemed like it was, it was two winters, that he was flat on his back all winter was when I said it. Well, when he bought this land, this crop I was telling you about, where he made his start, he harvested, and he hoiled wheat by his shavier yon, and he hauled all the sacks off to work this shit off, and I think that's how it started.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Anyway, there were two winters, he laid flat on his back all winter, rheumatism.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have any, you had a sister, your brother was younger, and you were the oldest boy?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I was the oldest boy, and my sister was older, and I was next. Then there's three years difference between me and my next brother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your sister, how much older?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Two years older than I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is she helping the work that you did?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, no, she didn't do too much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it all fell on you pretty much, didn't it?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much could the seven year old boy do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I did all the pollen. We couldn't do that, that's the only thing. I had, tell you what you had, we had five horses, I drove five horses to the breast, on a gangpile.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'm surprised that you couldn't even handle them. Yeah, I do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had you watched your father work much before?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah, oh yeah, sure, sure. Well, he told me what to do and how to do it from his bed.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But I didn't do no sheathing because I couldn't lift the grain up and put them in the river.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I like to get the pollen and holler and such, is that what I can do?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I'm used to, I've heard before about boys starting to do men's work when they're about ten, really. But it sounds like you got started a little young. Yeah, and then I was, for me, I was always a small for my age.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But, those days the little kids, I was going to say, it was nice to see little kids riding the teams and such as that. They grew up on a farm, you know, they learn and they learn fast.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Just like now, ladies, little young kids drive me to big trackers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember much about school?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yes, I remember quite a little bit about school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: First school I went to was Aspenville Schoolhouse, down there. Well, there's this side of Lenville down here that comes here. Yeah, about eighty -five kids went to that running room school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: One teacher.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And, in about 1934, I guess it was, my daughter went to the same school, started at the same school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With eighty -five kids and one teacher, how could you learn anything?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: as I said, we didn't have too many months school. And, yes, usually,

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: a kid was about seventeen or eighteen years old before he got through the eighth grade. Because, those days, the boys had to stay home and help farm. That's one reason they had to start years of school, because they had to work on the farm in the fall, work on the farm in the spring, and so, therefore, they didn't get in much school. And, when I went to school down there, there were fellows going to school down there that was just as big or as big as my father. Other than men, I guess men that were eighteen, seventeen, eighteen years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn much in school, or was it hard to learn with so many kids?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't know, I guess it's just like now, if you want to learn, you got what you put into it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't say that I was such a good student, I guess I was average, but my sister, she was a good student. And, there's a few others in the class, same as I said, there's good students, and there's some of them, and I know kids down there that were in my class and started to feel, look like they were fifteen, sixteen year olds, she was a big kid. And, I went into second and third grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about play? What would you do to have fun playing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah. They used to, they used to play. The play that I remember the most was Cacti Whip.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You line up a bunch of guys, lift all the big guys up on one end, and you get a swing and then run, and then they'd raise the step and go there, and if you were on the end, they'd just pop the tester wide open. Shoot. We'd play that, and they'd just play a run, shoot, run. And, we played baseball, and cricket.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Cricket. Cricket.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, we'd dig a little hole in the ground about a foot long, about three or four inches deep. And, see now you had to, then you lay a stick across this hole, about four, and then you take another stick and throw it, and whoever caught the stick, you had to lay the big stick you threw that with, lay it across that hole there, and this kid caught the stick, he threw it, and if he could hit the stick, you toss the little stick off, well then, it was his time to throw the stick, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most of the kids liked school pretty well?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think they did, and I think they liked school pretty well. I think they did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Those games were school games. Yeah, we played them at school.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah. Coming over here with the dinner bus coming to school. In those days, there's lots of, lots of families lived on 80 acres, farms, 40 acre farms. A 320 acre farm was a big farm in those days.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's an 80 acre farm.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, Mr. Cannon's place, I think there's, there's a hundred, 160, I believe a little over 160, but of course it wasn't all in cultivation. And the place where my dad lived on a long time, as we left the bad place, the Cannon's place, that was 160, and I think it was about 80 acres of that, it was about 80 acres of farmland, that's about all there was on it. Most of it was timber.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this schoolhouse get a lot of use for socializing activities?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that old Aspenville schoolhouse, when they consolidated, it was the second year my daughter went to school. She went to school there her first year. And the next year, the consolidator was Genesee, and then she went to Genesee there for a year or two on the way down there. And they let the old schoolhouse go to pot like they did most of the districts, rural districts. The families, they got together and fixed it up, and used it for kind of a community center, you know, they had different socials there. There was a gun club, they had a gun club there for quite a while, I don't think they had that anymore. We were down there, oh, it's been about four years ago I guess, since we was down there, we were down to one of the couples in the community that was celebrating their 50th wedding anniversary. We were down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: There were young married people, same time we were down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me they were mostly Irish down there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, all the further down there, Linville County, that's Irish little bit. You know, over there where we were, through there, coastal mountain over there, that's mostly Scandinavian. Oh, I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, I'm trying, where was Aspendale located now, from Linville?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it's this side of Linville. It's the first school, first school this side of Linville is Aspendale District. It's a schoolhouse right in Linville, and then you come on the street towards Moscow, and then there was the Aspendale District. Then you come on up, before you cross the mountain, a couple of places like that, they formed another district then, that was the Ferndale District. Then you cross the mountain lawn over here, where you get to Moscow, that was the Mount Tomar District over there, in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were going to school there in Aspendale, did they have a lot of community get -togethers?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yeah. We had most of it in the wintertime. In the wintertime we had literary, you know, we'd have programs, and every Saturday night we'd, I think about every Saturday night we'd have, gather down at the schoolhouse, and have literary kids who speak peaches, and we had debate teams, or debate, or something, going on, on Saturday nights. Christmas was always a big deal. In those days, everybody knew his neighbor.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I know there's one vacant house down there in our district, and there's about a few kids, my mother and I, they're 8 and 9, 10 years old, I don't know what that is. So we made a club out of this house, we used this little house for a club, and we would knock, we'd go down there at night, wintertime, build a fire, and read books. I remember when we were kids, maybe you remember about the serious or ... series, the serious or ... books, where we read, we always had a bunch of ... books, and we took turns about, reading.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You used to read a book at night?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: A book at night, yeah, we just passed it around. I read a while, and I passed over the next one, he'd read a while, and just kept it going, you know. And then, and we had one kid there, he was a pretty good tall kid, he's kind of tall -sized. And he was a pretty good cook. And what we'd do, probably tonight, one night, we'd go up to my folks' place, and go in the chicken house, and get a good old hen.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And of course, in those days, everybody had carrots, and rutabagas, and turns buried for falling.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But they'd dig them out of the garden in the fall, and they'd just dig a hole, and put straw in it, and cover them up, you know. And the people all winter, you'd go out there, and when the time was spent, dig a hole in there, reach in there, and you'd get a potato, or a rutabaga, or one of those buried there, you know, an apple. So we'd go get some carrots, and stuff, and he'd always make the mulligans, he'd call it, and we'd eat. Go on over, and probably next time, whatever, the other guy would go to one of the other guys' houses, and get a chicken.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you bother to tell the folks you were taking a chicken?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I think we told them after it was all over, that they told whoever it is. Of course, I guess, I think it kind of got out, that we was eating down there, and actually they wanted to worry.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And even though I was getting out no money, somebody was wondering where we were getting them chicken. I think it probably got out, because someone told it out. It sounded like pretty good, clean fun. Yeah, it was, it was, for a fact. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, did you call yourselves a club?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, we just, we just called our clubhouse, but we didn't have no name for the club then, we just gathered down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then in the summertime, we had this, we had a little Aspenville ball team, junior ball team, and we used to kind of play ball against Cornwall, and then Ville. We had lots of fun when I was a kid, out there in the country, of course. We used to come to town, one time I ever came to town, we'd come to town, was, circus come to town, actually it was a lot of once a year, circus come to town, and we, put up money enough to come to the circus.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what circuses were like here when you were young?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I remember the biggest ones that were here was Ringling Brothers, and there's Barnum and Bailey.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But Ringling Brothers was the biggest circus in here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And sometime after I got a little older, sometimes us kids, you know, not necessarily everybody had a saddle horse, a horse to ride anyway, and then there's times we used to ride to Moscow, and ride up Main Street and back, and going back home. I thought we had a lot of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Want some water?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, it's all right.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: What'd you do, you're going with your own place?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we did no work outside, taking care of her family. In fact, the better my mother and I ever were, she did very little work in the garden. My father did that, her work was, of course she had four boys, and she wanted to take care of them, and she did the worst of them, she might work in them. She did very little work outside, my mother did. And same with the, same with the, we had lots of flowers, and it seemed like my daddy was, he had his flower, man. Oh, she appreciated them, and we'd be out there with him a lot of time. She did, my mother did very little work outside of the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the same go for your sister, too?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she didn't do too much outside work, well, she got... ...pitched horses with us kids and things like that, and she rode horses back quite a little bit, but... ...she was mostly in the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that your father got a hard time very much because he was black?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't hardly think he did. I think it's yours.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't, I don't, I don't, I really don't think my father did. He wasn't that type of man, he seemed like it. He was a very, he was a likeable man, and he was a kind man, he was a good man, he was an honest man, and his word was good as gold. And so it, it seemed like that he blended in with the neighbors, the people, and carried the people. It's not like I say, when he was trying to raise money to buy this fella out of that property from this fella that way... ...he had a pretty hard time getting that because of the people, they didn't know him, they wouldn't trust him, they didn't know him as good or not. But finally somebody took a chance on him and did, and so, they actually got to know my dad right.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: It seemed like his color didn't hold him back much. Of course I imagine there was times that our color would get better if he was in another color, but...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: ...and for years, that dress boy got a pretty good size. As I said, he was on a hay baler, and he baled hay all over the country, and he always had all he could do.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I ran into Mr. Johnson, who lives down in Juliaetta.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Johnson?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Johnson, I think that's his name. He lived at Linville, Linville country. He told me he knew you and I should say hello to you for him. He said you and your father baled hay for him. I went to Johnson in the country, I wouldn't know who.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: My father baled hay all over the country. From the trip around Palouse up here, up to the top of the western grade, and then back down to Uniontown, and Genesee. He said, I'm going to retire me while we're baling hay. I left the baler and went in service, and then came back and started eating right where I left off. I was bailing when I came back.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Hard work. One thing I was thinking was that, I don't know if it's true, but it seems like in a pioneer kind of setting, it's easier maybe for a man to be judged just by what he's worth, and by what he does, not whether what his nationality is or his color. And I think that, I was wondering if you feel that way too, whether this kind of country in the west would be a better place for a man looking for opportunity than maybe other places. Well, I'll tell you, that is one reason that brought my father out to the west, because this little man that he worked for there, an enforcement officer, he told my dad in their conversation, he said, I think that would be a good place for you, you young man, you raised a family, and as far as I could fly and see, right up when I was out there, there was very little prejudice about in that part of the country. Racial prejudice, you see, that's what my father told my dad, he said, as far as he could see, he said, seen we are less racial prejudice, not like it is down there in the south. That's one reason that excited my dad too, that's one reason that prompted him to come west, besides what he wanted to get out. And another thing that, he thought there's lots of game out here, and I guess that's part of his Indian blood, and he liked to hunt. And that's what he did for us kids, well those kids, 7 or 8 years old. We used to walk through some mountains up here, he got him some hounds, and chase coyotes, hunt coyotes, and on these mountains, every weekend, instead of going downtown, where we'd get our guns and go to the hills. That's the way we spent our weekend up, so we were never going to get some. We never did have no accidents either, sometimes it would be as high as six of us kids together, us boys and some of our neighbor kids, rolling over in the yard. All was gone rough, we never did have no accidents either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, your father, know of any other black families around, in any words, in this inland country?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, when we came here, there was one more black family that was in here, was up here at Derry, by the name of Wells. They was in here when we came here. Now he was the only one, and then in about 1910, fellas, there was another black family that settled, that was coming up here at Ten Set, up there at his farm. The homeskated up there on that reservation up there, and there's some of them still up there, some of the boys, the girl and one of the boys right there, and one of the boys, two boys and a girl up there, yeah. The girl lives on Old Home's old home place, and the two boys have got places of their own up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's their name?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Caney.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Caney?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Caney, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the Wells?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, I knew the Wells, I knew them. I went up there, when I got out of the Army, I went up there and worked for a little man, Wells, and he was a little, he was a little organ up there, a spotlight, and I worked for a little fella up there for about a month, I guess, and I went up there and got in DC, I worked for him for about, I worked for him for about a couple of months, I think, in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was his wife, Lou, still alive then? Huh? Was his wife, Lou, still alive?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she's still alive, yes, she was still alive then, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about them?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I've heard a lot about them in DC. Well, I'll tell you.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They were a different type of people than my folks. They were hard workers, and they were hard drinkers. And my father, my family was, was not tight. They made good money and they sold it away. Of course, there were other poor people there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And anyway, he could have been a, he had a chance of being a rich housewife, but they just blew it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, they made good money, and they could have bought good land.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The chief at that time, and they was in there, you know, good farm out on the ridge, with the best land that ever rained on. But they stayed in the woods, they made good money in the woods, if they'd have sailed it, but they made it and they spent it. I've heard that Joe was real close to Frank and Crom Wells. Well, well, now if I, here's the way I heard it, that Frank and Crom Wells owned Joe's parents and slavery. Now I heard that, now I know how to, I must have heard that. And, of course, Frank and Crom and Joe, they knew each other, oh, when they was kids, you see. And when Frank and Crom came out here, they're the ones that sent for Joe. Got him out, got him out here, now that's the way I heard it, that's the way I heard it now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much truth do you think?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I can't swear to it, but I heard that's the way I heard it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it would have been Frank and Crom's parents that owned Joe's parents?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, that's what I heard, that's what I heard, and of course, that owned Joe's parents, see. And Joe was born on their place, and of course Frank and Crom was kids too, and all kids together there on the same plantation. And, well, Frank and the young folks owned the plantation and owned the same. Now that's what I heard. I can't take away that through and through. Well, I've heard the same thing. I've heard that they were just like brothers. Yeah, they were good, yes they were, thank you. Well, see, that's one reason, that's one thing that they had the advantage of my dad when he came out here. Because these wells, they had money, see. They helped old Joe. Well, my dad, he came out here, he didn't have no money, I think when he landed there he had to borrow as many kids as he had dollars.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I'm not quite sure what he said. You know, funny thing about Joe Wells is that he's got a heck of a reputation in Derry as being a, in Loon too, as being a great family. They get a lot of respect in Derry. Yes they are, yes they are.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They like old Joe up there, sure they do, don't they?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I guess the old guy, he had a heart as big as a hotel, but that part's concerned. And old Lou, she's, I like her, she's just a good old soul. I like the old Lou. You remember her pretty well. Oh, I remember her pretty well, of course I boarded with them when I worked up there, I do her pretty well. She's the only one that I cared for, an old lady, I like the old lady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she still cooking her own?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, yeah. I've heard tell her cooking was really special. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, she's a good cook, I have her. When they had that, they had that, they kept the cabin and a lot of people stopped on their way to the cabin. They were all in the back of the house at that time, and people going up in the white finders to just stay all night there and stop there and get a meal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And in early days, yeah, he, who else he known for and why?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He helped build some of these buildings, big buildings here in Moscow, and in Colfax too, and he's come to Parshcom out here.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I don't know when they came here, but I know they were here when we came here, they must have come in probably in the early part of the 90's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know if Lou and Joe had been married back east before they came here?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, they was married before they came out here, yeah, they was married. Cause I didn't know if he met her out here or back there. No, they was married back there, because old Joe, old Joe come out here, I heard him tell that to himself. He came out here, the Wells boys brought him out here, and I don't know how many of those kids he's born by. Anyway, anyway, he come out here, and they sent me, they sent money back to her.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He sent for rafters after he came out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She died first didn't she?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, she was the first one who died.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I remember people have said that she was a very specially nice person, very kind person. Yes she was, she was well as a prisoner so I liked her, I liked her better than any of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were Chuck and Roy older than you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, they were older than me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any good horses?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I think old Chuck, old Manuel, he had some good horses up there in the woods when they logged up there and got them. They had some, well the pot I stayed too with. He had horses just as good as the Potlatch. He had four he had nice horses, way close to a ton a piece.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's saying quite a bit, the potlatch had good horses. Yeah, that's when they logged the horses, you know, did all this getting the horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it work when you were working for him? Did he just get contracts to log?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he was a gyppo one. See the potlatch had let small tracks of timber out on the gyppo jobs, you know, lots of gyppo logs up there and so on. Old Joe and Chuck, he stayed up there and Roy, he was a sheep man. He was in there in the summertime, he was off the sheep. But Chuck and his dad, they did the gyppoing for the potlatch. I don't know why the potlatch did it, but they'd hire a patch to tell them whether it was too small for them to fool with their outfit or what. But then they let it out. They'd get full loggers. But it kept going that long for potlatch and they'd get pretty good.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I heard Joe had a pretty big operation too, I mean for that time. Yeah, yes he did. Yes he did, that's what I said. They made good money. They made good money. Those kids, I can remember saying to old Joe, telling him and himself that Chuck and Roy had worked and I would get $500 a piece and Joe would smoke can, spend the weekend, and old Joe would have to send them money to come home.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: That's the old lumberjack, that's what that is. I've heard that story. Yeah, yes that's old Joe. They'd go and go to Spokane, $500 a piece in their pocket and buy a gully. And you'd have to send money up to them to come home. So they wrote a lot of good money to send out there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he had one daughter, Mary. Yeah, uh -huh. I heard that Mary was the valedictorian at Derry. That's what someone said. She was a really good student there. Well, she might have been, I don't know. She had a daughter that was pretty bright, pretty good. Well, she was a good student.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, she was a good student. I don't know. She might have been, Mary might have been. I don't know. Of course, when I knew them, they were all grown. I was growing too, but when I was a kid I never knew. The only time our families was, the two families was together, that's when Lou died. Our whole family went up to her funeral. And she just passed away.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But outside of that, we never saw too much of each other. Of course, old Chuck worked for my dad on the heavy or something.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Then he got to be an old man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Roy stay in the country there even though he was her in the shade?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: At the same time, he was there quite a little bit. But anyway, he was some big sheep man down there on the river. He worked with sheep all the time. And I've heard stories about both Chuck and Joe. And Mary had a boy that he took after his Uncle Roy. He went to sheep too. He was down in Arizona down there. After Roy had passed away, I heard that's what he was doing down in Arizona there. He was working with sheep. I've heard that Joe, I've heard stories about what a strong man Joe was. He was a strong man. But he wasn't very big. No, he was a small man. He was a good man. Even Chuck, Chuck was a husky man for his size. But old Joe, he was a strong man. He was a good worker too. But I say they...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: If he just had a little better management, he'd have been a rich man. I heard one story about Chuck that he is so strong that he'd just break peaty handle after peaty handle on logs and landings. Well, he probably could.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I know there's a certain way you're handling it. You can make it easier. I know that's why they had a hard time with me. You know, they're hollering at me about the way I handled the peaty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you did when you were at work for them?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I did swamping. It was my job, swamping, because I got the chance to handle peaty some too. But swamping was my job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big was the crew when you were there?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, it wasn't much of a crew. I think there's two. There's two fellows doing the sawing.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And I think myself and another guy most of the time was doing the swamping. I swamped the study and there was another fellow that had swamped. Part of the time when they got in hard, they lost the following. And old Chuck and Joe, they did the skid. And there was about six of us together. Two sawers and two on the swamp. Part of the time Chuck and Joe, they did the skid. And then the one guy worked down on the landing there quite a little bit. When he wasn't helping the swamp while he worked down there, he had a crew of about six.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was they using horses then?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yeah, they were using horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far would they have to go with the timber? Just get on the landing?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, just get on the landing. I imagine somebody had a skid a quarter of a mile. Maybe a quarter of an hour. You didn't stay at that work too long. No, no I stayed at it for about a part of December I think. And then that's when my mother and dad went south. And I said my dad would go to my house where my grandmother left us off my dad sent for me to come home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your dad went back south to live?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, he went back down there and spent the winter. He built a home for my grandmother and helped his sister go on with the interview. He spent most of the winter down there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Two months or better anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that your father sold vegetables in Moscow?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, he had out here, they bought 20 acres out here and he had lots of berries and he always had a big garden and different kinds of berries and And of course after people knew we had them and they'd call up home on that order. And that's the one time my mother worked outside more than anything else. She worked outside in the berry, getting the berries ready. Kids picked them and she'd get the crates ready and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you stay in school until you got through the 8th grade? How long did you stay in school?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I stayed my junior year. My first year I graduated from the business department of Moscow High School in 1914. And then I decided to go on. I got... oh, I went to my junior year.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I figured to go on before I left the one after I finished first year from business, a business course. And I decided to go on to high school long enough to pick up enough credit so I could get in college. I wouldn't have had that. I wouldn't have had that. I could have got 28 credits if I had gotten in college, you see. Then I started high school kind of late in life because I lived out in the country when I finished 8th grade.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: About two years after my 6th grade before I got to Tammany High School, so I was getting up pretty good, well up in age myself. And the war broke out, so that kind of hit me.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And when I got out of the army, I...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I could have went first, but instead of that I took a correspondence course and decided to go into civil service.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Rio de Mayo Creek is where I had mine.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And when I got the call, I took the examination when I got the call, my wife and I there, we were going to get married and she thought she didn't like getting tied here. So, we were going to a male Rio de Mayo Creek, I'd be going away from home all the time. And she thought it would be better if we just went to the farm and went to the farm, so that's what we did.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: The corn wall was quite a little place for a kid when I was a kid. There was two stores there and a back -and -forth shop and a farm there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the 4th of July's like over there? Huh? What were the 4th of July's like?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, well, we had horse races and a baseball game and foot races for the kids and stuff like that, you know. There was lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Speeches too? Were they speeches?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, I don't remember too many speeches. I'll be the last one, last one I had out there though. I think it was about time World War I broke out. Well, then, you know, somebody gave a big speech that time, I can't remember who it was. I just took my mind because we had a big, somebody give a talk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But this baseball, how did that work?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Was it a league or was it just an informal, just a... Just the kids from different localities get together. That's where those kids, Ashford, the other kids around Ashford, in that district. Team East Cornwall, of course, Cornwall, they had a little bit of drawing, a little more drawing power than most of them. But they didn't have any better team than we did, I don't believe. But that's what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there would really be quite a few people come watching?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yes, I'll say so. You bet. Just like I said, just like there's a big league game.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And some of those old farmer boys played pretty good ball too. The kids. Of course, you take, well, we had like Blaine and Thornfist in there. They had baseball teams too, but they were men. They were going up, so they had a good game. But Cornwall and Linville and Ashford, the other way, we were just teenager kids playing. And we had some pretty good players on the whole team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What position did you play?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I played a little bit of first base, but I was pretty short for first base. I liked first base the best. I liked playing first base.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Short stop is where I played most of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was pretty much the same way as they are now?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, we played. You know, of course, we didn't have no back zone. Well, in part, you just...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: You used to have as high as your head and went across the plate, why, he called it a strike.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, it was right on the ground, and he didn't hit the ground and bounced over the catcher's head, and he caught away. That was a strike too. He told them to get him over the plate, well, that was a strike. Oh, and I don't know, it didn't matter too much.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: But it worked the same way as both sides, so I guess we can't get.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the games pretty wild and wooly? Were they high -scoring games? Can you remember?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, they weren't too high -scoring games. They weren't too high -scoring games. Various, various, various elements got a home run. Sometimes some kid get up there and get knocked home by them, and help them. Most of the time get out there in the weeds and lose the ball. Of course, we had some of them pretty good, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a ball would you use? You didn't have hard balls, did you?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah. That was before softball, softball came into picture, yeah. No, we wasn't playing when softball came. I played some softball, but that was after I came to town there. But those days we played hard ball.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about gloves?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well... We had all kinds of gloves.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: We had gloves, some weren't very good.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And we had a hard time keeping a mask. We had a mask, but I don't think there was much time we didn't have a dress protector.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And we played some games. I remember when we didn't have a mask. And nobody got hurt.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: So you figured they had good crowds because... Yeah, we had good crowds. They had good crowds because there wasn't no place else to go. No, they just finally took up the whole hacking team, and come over and watch the kids play ball. And they'd get a chance to visit with all the neighbors too, you know. They had a lot of fun out of it as we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be mostly during summer?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, during summer, after school was out. Between school time and harvest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about betting? Did they bet on the games?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: No, I don't remember doing much betting on the games. Well, now that you guys had played, I was thinking of that. No, we didn't. We didn't do much gambling. They didn't fight over it either, but you know, when Si lost. Up in Princeton, Harvard, they didn't seem to... They didn't come to blows over some of those games. Yeah, I get up there on the little wood ranch, you know, and they get up there and they get the... ...the baseball and the licks are mixed together, moonshine mixed together there, and they get in some wild time sometimes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the fall, did they have a lot of auctions?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, we've had lots of auction fields down here in the fall of the year.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I was wondering if that wasn't the same kind of thing. It'd be a place where people would get together. Yeah, yeah, they did. You take that to harvest time.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: See, like every weekend, there's an auction sale on some farm around somewhere in the little country. And every billboard you see it have an auction sale on it. Some people have an auction sale. Some farm had lost their lease or something and fell out. Maybe retire. Retire. And then after...

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And after the tractor started coming in, some farmer in the locality, he'd get a tractor. And everybody in the neighborhood that owned land and had a reindeer on it, farming with horses, that's why then you didn't want that guy as the tractor to farm his place, and then the fellow horses would get the haul. That's why the big farmers got started. And that was the end of the little farm when the tractor started coming in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why? Just because the tractor could do so much more?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, faster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was lots of turnover of renters then when the tractor was coming?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Mm -hmm. Yeah. As I said while ago, before the tractor came in, there's lots of 160 and 80 and 40 acre farms. The family lives on the tractor, and they're all gone now. This kid here together, I went to school with his older sisters down there this afternoon at Myrtle Smits. You might know him. He lived down in Aspen Dale district down in there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He started out 160, and now he got back down in that neighborhood with all horse farmers. There was only two of them. There was two of them that had practice, but they got old and they let him in. Now he's got 1400 acres down there. Of course, there's two of the fellas that got practice. Now they got old and retired, and they let him have his farm, their farm. Ray Campbell and Ben Joann.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most people go to tractors really quick, or did the horses hold out for very long?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: They didn't hold out too long. I think it's now they were... See, I started farming in 24. The tractors were just coming in then.

speaker: Eugene Settle
