words: I quit farming when I was 35. In just about 10 years from that time, there was just very few horse farmers. Very few horse farmers. And then the farms were getting bigger all the time then too.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Now I farm. I farm with horses.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: 240 in one place, and then I rent another place on 160, of course there's only about 7 acres of it in cultivation. I farm with horses, and I was putting a big side of farming on it.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: I couldn't keep it though, because the tractors got it. When I got a tractor, before I got a tractor, then I lost some of the ground that I was farming with horses, so I didn't have too much.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And then another thing, these tractor farmers, they started buying land too. We took the mineeber down that coastal farm we had. Westbury Brothers, there's four boys up there, they lost their father. They had a good stand -in with the first bank of Troy down there, and he passed an old rocky back down there. And they started buying land, and they had a tractor, and they started buying land. I don't know how much land they have got down there now. They're the Westbury Brothers. Big farmers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, having the tractors, that just gave them a real jump on the horses?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Oh yes, it did. They just gave them all kinds of jump on the farm. Because if you had a tractor, there was no trouble for you to rent land, you see. You had a big spread. You got advantage of the little guy. As I say, if a fellow owned them, was renting 160, he's farming with horses, and you got up in there with a big tractor and going down there and doing the work. And during the time the fellow was putting the horse, you'd rent the fellow at least your place to the man at the tractor. And it seemed like in the later years the season started getting shorter too. We used to farm with horses, you know, we used to get out there in the spring and start in March. And we were up the first of May before we'd get the crop in. But now, that's on 160. If you get down there, you can take down all the big tractor like a shoe. These farmers don't make no difference. I must have been farming for about 10 days in the air. They got a shoe because they have to make equipment just like Berta Spence. He's got, he's been farming about 1400 acres, but he's got two of those great big real tractors, you know. I think he said they pull, I think he told me if he pulls, that he'd pull 40 foot of spring two harrow behind what I was doing. You know, when you go to take 40 foot swats around 160 acres, you don't make many swats.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: In the same way, the same way as your drills, I imagine he pulls four drills. Well, there's another 40 foot saw. I tell you right now on how, I guess he can just pull all he can make, hook together and how. You don't stay out there. When you go out to put in your spring crop, you don't stay out there all summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of investment was it for a guy to go to a tractor from a horse team?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Well, to start with, now most of the fellas that went to tractors early, tractor companies took lots of times they bought a lot of their horses, took horses in on them. They went out there and got a seat on the loom down there. They'd take your horses in on a tractor. Now what they did, well from there I don't know what the hell it got to it. I mean lots of times, a fella would go by a tractor and have an auction sale and sell off his horses. He would buy good horses for a little to nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much were the tractors running in those days? Do you remember?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yes, yes sir. My neighbor down there, one of my neighbors bought a diesel, a first diesel that I didn't know of. It was a big diesel that came in there.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And he, I think he gave about $4 ,500 for it. And now they bought a 30 Caterpillar and I think he gave about $3 ,000 for it. And now the big diesel like this, the Spencer's got those big tractor wheel pushes, they're on about $45 ,000.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He used to tell me, he says, Gene, he says, how much money you have invested in machinery?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: He says, Gene, if I had them paid for and could get the money I got into them, he said, I could retire and I owe nothing to work with them. I said, yeah, I'm going to have $100 of these trees we had in just in machinery and all.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: And this fellow bought this first diesel down there, I think he gave close to $5 ,000 for it, at least that $4 ,000, $40 ,000 a year for it. And at that time, he could have bought a, you know, 160 for my wife and I had there. He gave $4500 a year for his tractor and we bought this 160 for $5 ,000. It was about 40 acres, there was about 100 acres of it to go to Leeson, I think it was about 35 or 40 acres, it was a scab jammer. I've heard that some of the old timers, guys that had been at it for a long time, just didn't want to go to the tractors from the horses. Oh, there they were, they had lots of them, just the same way about combines, a lot of these, those when combines first came out, oh no, why don't we just scatter weeds, see those. I don't want nothing to do with them. It wasn't long before we had them. This was when they pulled them by horses, a lot of the farmers wouldn't say they wouldn't have a combine on their place. And then the same thing about the tractors, they'd do something like they turned the dirt or turned it too fast or something, they'd just kick it again in the face and all come around and do it. I'm sure you've heard the stories about the guy was on a tractor and the first time he'd try and stop and he'd start yelling, whoa! Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Yeah, the small farmer don't have a chance now, they just, in fact that's about the only fella that's making any money, that's the big farmer, that is, it's like a bird all down there. And now he's got all that land and he's got, he bought these steel tanks and he's got his own farm storage there and he's giving himself in pretty good shape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the beginning of the end of the small farming was when the wheel tractors came?

speaker: Eugene Settle
words: Yeah, when the tractor started coming in, that is all about, that is, that was all about the first part of the 20s.
